glossary.definition: The concept of artificial intelligence is considered in connection with our understanding of intelligence, focusing on how to assess it.
glossary.sentence: In this topic, you will find out what we understand by intelligence, how artificial intelligence is defined, and what is a fundamental approach to assessment of intelligence of a computer system.

glossary.definition: Systems designed to support human tasks and solve various problems
glossary.sentence: We need to make a transition from the real world to the world of intelligent computer systems.

glossary.definition: Agents that act to achieve the best outcome or, when uncertain, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome...

glossary.definition: A test used to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence by comparing responses to those of humans.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: Process of maintaining successful communication with the tester as the human being does.
glossary.sentence: Natural language processing for maintaining successful communication with the tester...

glossary.definition: Storing information acquired before and during the test.
glossary.sentence: Knowledge representation, in order to store information acquired before and during the test.

glossary.definition: Long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge and short-term memory to store knowledge processed at a specific time.
glossary.sentence: Humans use for this purpose long-term memory to store all previously acquired knowledge...

glossary.definition: Using stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: Automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions...

glossary.definition: Ability of a system to adapt to new situations and acquire patterns from different situations.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning, in order to be able to adapt to a new situation, as well as to acquire and generalize patterns...

glossary.definition: Capability to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: The regular Turing test excludes direct physical interaction between the computer and the tester, because it is not necessary to simulate a human being...

glossary.definition: Capability to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: The regular Turing test excludes direct physical interaction between the computer and the tester, because it is not necessary to simulate a human being...

glossary.definition: Contribution of feedback theory, suggesting intelligent behavior results from feedback mechanisms.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory...

glossary.definition: Research on how the human brain processes information, leading to artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience - Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: Mathematical formalization required for reasoning and probabilistic judgments.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas...

glossary.definition: Theories on how to make choices leading to desired results.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results...

glossary.definition: Research questions related to human and animal thinking and action.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.definition: Development of artefacts enabling artificial intelligence applications.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.definition: Feedback theory contributing to understanding intelligent behavior through simulation.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory...

glossary.definition: Study of language use in relation to thinking, contributing to natural language processing.
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation...

glossary.definition: Scientific and engineering goals for understanding intelligent performance in systems.
glossary.sentence: Goals of artificial intelligence can be considered along two dimensions: scientific goal is related to understanding the principles which make possible intelligent performance...

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems
glossary.sentence: Symbolic representation of knowledge is not much learnable

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons)
glossary.sentence: Connectionist representation of knowledge proves to be learnable

glossary.definition: Solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence solves the problem but is not able to effectively reason the solution

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence with a very good sense of reasoning for the solved problem
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence have a very good sense of reasoning for the solved problem

glossary.definition: Approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated

glossary.definition: Intelligence that surpasses human capabilities in practically every field
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities

glossary.definition: Focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena at multiple spatial and temporal scales
glossary.sentence: Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment

glossary.definition: Paradigm under new artificial intelligence where collective intelligence emerges through interaction and cooperation of agents
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence

glossary.definition: Probabilistic algorithm inspired by ant behaviour when searching for food
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization, which studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by ant behaviour

glossary.definition: Probabilistic algorithm inspired by birds and fish
glossary.sentence: It includes particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish

glossary.definition: Symbols used in the context of computer science
glossary.sentence: symbols and search

glossary.definition: The process of finding information or solutions
glossary.sentence: to cope with different types of problems. However, this is not a trivial task...

glossary.definition: Ways of organizing data about the real world
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world.

glossary.definition: Places where data is stored
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.

glossary.definition: Methods or procedures for processing data
glossary.sentence: we need to think about: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: A sequence of actions to reach the goal state
glossary.sentence: Once we have defined the problem, we can start thinking about resources and opportunities available...

glossary.definition: Identifying the state we are in and the goal state
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present... and a state we would like to reach.

glossary.definition: the starting condition of the game
glossary.sentence: blank grid

glossary.definition: The desired outcome of the problem-solving process
glossary.sentence: So, in your case, the goal state is the employer's office at location B.

glossary.definition: Discrepancy between initial and goal states
glossary.sentence: Since you are still in your apartment, there is a discrepancy between the initial and goal states of the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: Graph representing all possible states of the game
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game to all goal states.

glossary.definition: Actions that change the state in the problem-solving process
glossary.sentence: Each action changes a state of the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: Process of connecting puzzle fragments to form a complete picture
glossary.sentence: When assembling a puzzle, we are coming close to a whole picture.

glossary.definition: states where three marks are in a row, column or diagonal
glossary.sentence: three marks in a row, column or diagonal

glossary.definition: the player starting the game
glossary.sentence: player with X

glossary.definition: states in the game process
glossary.sentence: discrete states of the game

glossary.definition: moves that can be made according to the rules of the game
glossary.sentence: placing marks in empty squares

glossary.definition: sequence of moves from one state to another
glossary.sentence: process of changing between game states

glossary.definition: different levels in the state space graph representing different stages
glossary.sentence: levels showing moves from initial to final states

glossary.definition: moves made by players in a turn-based game
glossary.sentence: players alternate turns

glossary.definition: game where one player's gain is the other's loss
glossary.sentence: deterministic two-player game principle

glossary.definition: properties that define a state in the game
glossary.sentence: values that change after a move

glossary.definition: visual representation of the game state
glossary.sentence: display of marks on the grid

glossary.definition: number representing a player's points
glossary.sentence: tracking progress in the game

glossary.definition: measure of difficulty or computational demand of the state space
glossary.sentence: avoid overconsumption of resources

glossary.definition: data structure showing possible game states
glossary.sentence: organized representation of game states

glossary.definition: method to find a solution in the state space
glossary.sentence: algorithm for solving problems

glossary.definition: variety of diagnostic tests that can be applied
glossary.sentence: necessary to confirm or deny a hypothesis about the patient's diagnosis

glossary.definition: All of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical because uninformed search algorithms process only nodes and arcs.
glossary.sentence: In the case of uninformed search, all of the paths represented in the state space graph are identical...

glossary.definition: Both algorithms considered so far—Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search—explored the state space graph through a principle of deepening.
glossary.sentence: Let’s consider another way of searching the state space graph.

glossary.definition: The algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: However, this algorithm is not guaranteed to find the shortest path to the search goal node, if such a path exists in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Refers to situations where the algorithm searches for long depths before returning.
glossary.sentence: The reason is that the algorithm always goes deeper into the state space graph and finds the first path to the search goal node, which may not be the shortest one.

glossary.definition: A modification of Depth-First Search that prevents going along indefinitely deep paths.
glossary.sentence: To overcome this shortcoming, a modification of Depth-First Search is offered. Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: The depth limit defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored.
glossary.sentence: The depth limit should be provided before the algorithm starts its operation.

glossary.definition: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes.
glossary.sentence: Nodes located at the depth limit are considered to be leaf/root nodes (depending on the search direction).

glossary.definition: An analogy used to explain the difference between Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search.
glossary.sentence: The analogy with the tree crown used before makes evident the difference between Depth-First Search and Depth-Limited Search.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that includes an additional operational step. It checks for common nodes in both OPEN lists to determine if a common node is found.
glossary.sentence: In each iteration, after expanding the current nodes, it checks if there is at least one common node in both OPEN lists.

glossary.definition: Every individual has different experiences, and rules based on heuristic knowledge are inherently individual.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic knowledge is applied in many areas of computer science.

glossary.definition: A mathematical function that helps the search algorithm determine the direction of the location of the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: This function helps the search algorithm to find a problem solution with minimum costs.

glossary.definition: A function that is not well-defined
glossary.sentence: On the other hand, if the function is not well-defined, Hill Climbing has several shortcomings...

glossary.definition: An example of data-driven search on the state space graph
glossary.sentence: state space graph

glossary.definition: The initial node in the search process
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A.

glossary.definition: The target node the algorithm aims to reach
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is K.

glossary.definition: The number of nodes kept in the search at each step
glossary.sentence: The beam width is 2 nodes.

glossary.definition: Values used to evaluate nodes in the search process
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are given close to nodes.

glossary.definition: List of nodes that have been expanded
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far.

glossary.definition: List of nodes that are currently being considered for expansion
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A.

glossary.definition: A step in the search process
glossary.sentence: Iteration 0: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A state in the search space
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: Process of implementing a game using an intelligent computer system
glossary.sentence: To implement a game, it is not sufficient to represent the game using a state space graph.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that use heuristics to guide the search
glossary.sentence: The selection of an algorithm for the implementation of a deterministic two-player zero-sum game depends on the availability of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: Process of simulating game behavior to achieve a win
glossary.sentence: To implement a game, it is necessary to equip an intelligent computer system with an algorithm that will process the graph.

glossary.definition: Applications of search-based algorithms in game design
glossary.sentence: Use cases A complete state space graph is typically used in simple games with a small number of states.

glossary.definition: Rules that define how the game state changes during play
glossary.sentence: The state-transition rules of the game can be formalised.

glossary.definition: A graph where not all states are reachable from the root node
glossary.sentence: Partial state space graph means only some nodes can be reached after n moves

glossary.definition: A computer system that selects moves leading to the best state
glossary.sentence: heuristic measure

glossary.definition: A significant problem with limited look-ahead in heuristic evaluation
glossary.sentence: The depth of look-ahead may not detect promising paths

glossary.definition: A method that explores partial state space graph by increasing depth limits
glossary.sentence: Used when time limit exists for next move

glossary.definition: A heuristic evaluation function used in decision-making
glossary.sentence: Used to evaluate nodes in game tree search

glossary.definition: A technique to optimize minimax algorithm by pruning branches
glossary.sentence: Reduces computation during search process

glossary.definition: A structured sequence of moves in a competitive activity
glossary.sentence: Context for heuristic evaluation in decision making

glossary.definition: a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values
glossary.sentence: A data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A parameter that is used in the learning process
glossary.sentence: However, if you are interested in more information on gradient descent, please use these sources

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the testing of the model
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: configuration variables that are set by the developer before training
glossary.sentence: Step 5 is about becoming aware of the data collected. Usually, the data are summarised and visualised to identify missing or invalid data, the prevalence of classes, etc.

glossary.definition: the process of replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data
glossary.sentence: Data pre-processing (Step 4) typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data.

glossary.definition: internal configuration variables of the model whose value is found (learned) by a machine learning algorithm during the training
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: One machine learning algorithm can often simplify code and perform better.

glossary.definition: three main types of machine learning algorithms
glossary.sentence: There are three main types of machine learning algorithm s (Figure 6): supervised learning; unsupervised learning; reinforcement learning.

glossary.definition: a type of learning where input data already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer
glossary.sentence: Supervised learning is also called learning with a teacher. It is a type of explicit learning.

glossary.definition: a type of learning without a teacher, based on unlabelled data
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning is also called learning without a teacher. This type of learning is based on unlabelled data.

glossary.definition: carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (continuous)
glossary.sentence: Regression is carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: Task of attributing a data object to a specific class or category
glossary.sentence: In a classification task, it is necessary to attribute a data object to a specific class/category

glossary.definition: Dealing with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters
glossary.sentence: Clustering deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: a prediction made by the linear regression model
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: A value of the cost function across the whole dataset
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset

glossary.definition: The weight values of the linear function
glossary.sentence: input values are weight values of the linear function

glossary.definition: Presented by a feature vector
glossary.sentence: independent variables are presented by a feature vector

glossary.definition: Technique to minimise the cost function according to changes in the values of the weights
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised

glossary.definition: A hyperparameter that controls how quickly weights change during learning.
glossary.sentence: a learning rate is one of the hyperparameters used in artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: Accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm used for classification tasks
glossary.sentence: Logistic Regression Before talking about logistic regression, it is necessary to understand what the task of classification is

glossary.definition: When there are only two possible classes/categories represented by labels 0 and 1.
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).

glossary.definition: When there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong.
glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used.

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning where the model is trained using labeled data.
glossary.sentence: In a supervised machine learning task, the dataset contains data objects together with the right output labels.

glossary.definition: The linear function used in logistic regression
glossary.sentence: For example, based on data about the house area, number of floors, and land area under the house, it predicts the house price

glossary.definition: Function used to squash the value of the linear function within the range of [0,1]
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function

glossary.definition: The range within which the threshold is set for classification.
glossary.sentence: the threshold value usually is set in the range [-0.5; 0.5]

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks
glossary.sentence: The k-Nearest Neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm

glossary.definition: A metric used to determine the similarity of data objects
glossary.sentence: It applies a similarity metric such as the distance between data objects

glossary.definition: Labels that indicate the category or class to which a data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: True labels are given in the dataset and are used to determine the correctness of the predicted class labels.

glossary.definition: Input data for logistic regression
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression also tries finding a mathematical function that relates x to y. It uses the linear function as the linear regression does.

glossary.definition: Input data for logistic regression
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression in this case would determine the chances of this student to pass or fail the study course.

glossary.definition: Determines how similar data objects are based on feature descriptions.
glossary.sentence: the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects

glossary.definition: Feature values that can be measured on a continuous scale.
glossary.sentence: For continuous feature values, it is possible to calculate the geometric distance between any pair of data objects

glossary.definition: Measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i-th feature of a new data object and the value of the i-th feature of an existing data object.
glossary.sentence: The Euclidean distance measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object

glossary.definition: Calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.
glossary.sentence: Another distance metrics for continuous feature values is the Manhattan distance

glossary.definition: Calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used

glossary.definition: Datasets that include both continuous and categorical features.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data

glossary.definition: Calculates the distance for mixed-type feature values by summing the squares of differences.
glossary.sentence: The sum of the squared distances along corresponding features is used to measure similarity.

glossary.definition: Shows examples of calculating Euclidean and Manhattan distances for two data objects.
glossary.sentence: Figure 12 shows the examples of calculation of the Euclidean and Manhattan distances for two data objects

glossary.definition: A task of supervised machine learning where actual class labels are given and used to determine the correctness of predicted class labels.
glossary.sentence: The classification task generally demands predicting a class/category to which a particular data object belongs.

glossary.definition: On the one hand, the kNN algorithm is simple enough and does not have many hyperparameters that should be tuned.
glossary.sentence: On the one hand, the kNN algorithm is simple enough and does not have many hyperparameters that should be tuned.

glossary.definition: A parameter that defines the number of nearest neighbors used in the kNN algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: The probability of incorrectly classifying a new data object.
glossary.sentence: Deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.

glossary.definition: A measure of the accuracy of classification or regression models.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm will return the mean of the k labels for regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with one cluster and recursively splits it into smaller clusters.
glossary.sentence: Divisive hierarchical clustering applies a top-down clustering approach.

glossary.definition: A group of data objects that are similar to each other in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine how different two clusters are.
glossary.sentence: The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity.

glossary.definition: Different approaches used to define how clusters are merged during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: According to (Hastie, 2017) , different linkage methods lead to different clusters, and thus the choice of the method depends on the developer.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine the distance between clusters in hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The most used methods are (Hastie, 2017) : Complete-linkage, Single-linkage, Average-linkage.

glossary.definition: A graphical representation of hierarchical clustering showing the merging of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A visual representation showing how clusters are formed during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Figure 4 represents the simplified idea of divisive hierarchical clustering: data objects having the largest distance from other data objects in a cluster are split in a separate cluster at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: A linkage method where the distance between two clusters is the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Different linkage methods lead to different clusters, and thus the choice of the method depends on the developer. A dendrogram is an output of hierarchical clustering. It is a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: A measure used to determine similarity between clusters during hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: According to (Hastie, 2017) , a dendrogram provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format.

glossary.definition: A group of data objects that are connected in a dendrogram and share similar characteristics.
glossary.sentence: From the dendrogram in Figure 5, data objects C and E are the most similar in terms of the distance between them, as the height of the clade connecting them together is the smallest.

glossary.definition: The process of evaluating different levels of cluster cuts to determine the optimal number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: As a result, to make the right cut-off, the developers usually need to use evaluation metrics evaluating the algorithm's performance with the different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: A vertical line drawn through a dendrogram to determine the number of clusters.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut-off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: Calculated in the following way: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.
glossary.sentence: It is calculated in the following way: Mean absolute error (MAE) is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values.

glossary.definition: Also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable. It is calculated in the following way: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination.

glossary.definition: A two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.
glossary.sentence: The mentioned outcomes are typically summarised in a confusion matrix (Kubat, 2017). It is a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category.

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.
glossary.sentence: A precision metric evaluates a fraction of predicted positives that are true positives.

glossary.definition: Evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Accuracy metric evaluates a fraction of correct predictions over a given set of data objects.

glossary.definition: Provides an example for the dataset containing 100 data objects, 95 of which labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1.
glossary.sentence: Kubat (2017) gives an example for the dataset containing 100 data objects, 95 of which labelled as belonging to Class 0, and 5 labelled as Class 1.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.
glossary.sentence: Structure of the biological neuron

glossary.definition: Mathematical model of a neuron defined by McCulloch and Pitts in 1943.
glossary.sentence: Structure of an artificial neuron

glossary.definition: Human brain cells that contain a cell body, dendrites, and an axon.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons.

glossary.definition: The layers of neurons between input and output layers.
glossary.sentence: The layers of neurons between input and output layers are called hidden layers.

glossary.definition: Does not contain computing neurons; receives inputs from the environment.
glossary.sentence: The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers.

glossary.definition: The final layer that produces the predicted output for classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: The output layer sends the numerical output produced by the neural network to the environment.

glossary.definition: Neurons that perform computations within the network.
glossary.sentence: The input layer does not contain computing neurons because it only receives inputs signals from the environment and passes them to the hidden layers. On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: A function used in hidden layers to combine inputs.
glossary.sentence: On the other hand, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: A function used in hidden layers to introduce non-linearity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the output and hidden layers contain computing neurons with summation and activation functions.

glossary.definition: The ability of artificial neural networks to process inputs simultaneously across all neurons in a layer.
glossary.sentence: Moreover, artificial neural networks have a parallel processing nature because all the neurons in a layer process their inputs simultaneously and independently.

glossary.definition: A type of network with connections only in one direction.
glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction.

glossary.definition: A network where outputs are fed back into inputs, creating a dynamic system.
glossary.sentence: A recurrent network feeds its outputs back into its inputs.

glossary.definition: A method to modify weights in the network based on errors.
glossary.sentence: Learning in artificial neural networks is related to modifying weights in some particular way.

glossary.definition: The ability of artificial neural networks to continue functioning despite damage or changes.
glossary.sentence: According to Borisovs et al., 1998, artificial neural networks are resistant to partially or entirely damaged processing units or connections between processing units and network operation changes very little.

glossary.definition: The idea that knowledge in the network is not stored in one place but distributed across it.
glossary.sentence: Ertel (2011) emphasizes that the structure of the brain is adaptive, and it changes itself on the fly according to the individual's activities and environmental influences.

glossary.definition: Values that represent the network's knowledge at a particular time.
glossary.sentence: Weights make the memory of the artificial neural network. Therefore their values represent a set of network's knowledge in a particular time moment.

glossary.definition: A method used to train the artificial neural network.
glossary.sentence: According to (Poole & Mackworth, 2010), artificial neurons are much simpler than their biological counterparts, and artificial neural networks typically contain many fewer than the approximately 1011 neurons in the human brain.

glossary.definition: A type of artificial neural network that processes inputs using weighted sums and activation functions.
glossary.sentence: the perceptron processes it based on the summation and activation functions available in the processing unit.

glossary.definition: The simplest and most commonly known type of artificial neural network with one processing layer.
glossary.sentence: A Single Layer Perceptron is the simplest and the commonly known type of artificial neural network, but its practical applicability is limited because it can learn only linearly separable functions.

glossary.definition: The ability to separate signal from background in a signal.
glossary.sentence: Perceptron Rosenblatt developed the perceptron model in the late 1950s. A Single Layer Perceptron is really m separate networks because each weight affects only one of the outputs; thus, there will be m separate training processes.

glossary.definition: The process of adjusting the weights in a neural network based on the error in predictions.
glossary.sentence: the learning has the following main steps

glossary.definition: A data sample with its real classification label given to the perceptron.
glossary.sentence: a training example with its real classification label (the real output)

glossary.definition: Adjustments made to the weights based on the difference between predicted and actual outputs.
glossary.sentence: the system checks if there is a difference between the predicted output and the real output; if the predicted output is different...

glossary.definition: One cycle through all training examples for learning purposes.
glossary.sentence: the number of epochs corresponds to the number of times that the network will work through the entire training dataset.

glossary.definition: A class of models inspired by biological neural networks, consisting of layers of interconnected nodes.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters...

glossary.definition: A basic type of feed-forward neural network with one or more hidden layers.
glossary.sentence: The multilayer perceptron is a basic type of feed-forward neural network...

glossary.definition: Standard configurations used in practical applications.
glossary.sentence: Commercial artificial neural networks incorporate three-four layers...

glossary.definition: Includes input layer, one or more hidden layers, and output layer with activation functions.
glossary.sentence: The architecture of such a neural network is given in Figure 7.

glossary.definition: An additional input to each neuron that allows the model to shift the activation function.
glossary.sentence: Each neuron is connected to all neurons of the next layer. Each processing unit calculates the weighted sum, including the bias input...

glossary.definition: The difficulty of modeling functions based on the number of inputs and layers.
glossary.sentence: Each next layer increases computational complexity exponentially.

glossary.definition: The algorithm used to adjust weights by propagating error backwards through the network.
glossary.sentence: The learning in multilayer perceptron is based on the backpropagation algorithm.

glossary.definition: A layer in a neural network that processes inputs before passing them to the next layer.
glossary.sentence: The hidden layer consists of computing neurons and usually performs the detection of features.

glossary.definition: The units in a neural network responsible for some fraction of the error in each output unit.
glossary.sentence: The training data do not say what value the hidden processing units should have, and each hidden processing unit is responsible for some fraction of the error in each of the output units.

glossary.definition: A collection of information that intelligent systems use to make decisions or solve problems.
glossary.sentence: How does knowledge get to the knowledge base?

glossary.definition: A structure used to store knowledge about objects or concepts
glossary.sentence: frames store knowledge about each object in one place in a structured way

glossary.definition: Properties of objects/concepts in the frame.
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value.

glossary.definition: Extended knowledge about slot values and how to process them.
glossary.sentence: Facets are an extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge...

glossary.definition: Contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects.
glossary.sentence: A class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind.

glossary.definition: A derived class frame from another class frame.
glossary.sentence: Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes.

glossary.definition: Represents detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.
glossary.sentence: An instance frame represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain.

glossary.definition: Relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame.
glossary.sentence: The generalization relation allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.

glossary.definition: Represents extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts.
glossary.sentence: In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.

glossary.definition: Defines any other type of relations by using the referencing principle.
glossary.sentence: A referencing principle is used to relate two frames. This means that a unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame.

glossary.definition: Allows creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: Frame systems are linked together into frame-systems. Thus, to create a knowledge base for the frame-based expert system, we need to define and interrelate frames.

glossary.definition: Procedures that are invoked as side effects of slot values in a frame-based expert system.
glossary.sentence: Demons are procedures that are triggered automatically when a slot value is accessed.

glossary.definition: A self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent system.
glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: The method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire.

Instructions:
1. Extract ALL terms and concepts from the text.
2. Define them using ONLY the provided text.
3. NO external knowledge. Synthesize exclusively from source.
4. DO NOT translate. Keep definitions in the source language.
5. If no terms found, return an empty list.
